skippedSources.name: MacroEdge (subscriber chat thread)
skippedSources.reason: Zero analytical content — a one-sentence teaser announcing a paid report drops Wednesday plus a subscribe CTA. No thesis, data, or claim to extract.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Goldman Sachs (Brian Garrett)
items.title: Hedge funds already sold 75% of the equity exposure they built since April
items.idea: Goldman's head of equity execution, Brian Garrett, says hedge funds have sold roughly 75% of the global equity exposure accumulated since the April lows, even as the equal-weighted S&P 500 hit fresh record highs this week. Positioning is far cleaner underneath the surface than the record-high index implies. Goldman is forecasting 22% y/y earnings growth this season, with options markets pricing unusually large single-stock moves.
items.angle: If earnings clear the aggressive 22% bar, this unwound positioning becomes fuel for a squeeze higher. If they don't, there's no cushion of forced buyers left underneath the tape.
items.tweetA: Everyone's staring at the S&P's record high and assuming crowding. Goldman says hedge funds already sold 75% of the exposure they built since April. The "euphoria" you're seeing in the index is actually a rebuilding trade waiting to happen if earnings hold.
items.tweetB: The setup here is binary, not bullish or bearish. 75% of post-April hedge fund longs are gone. Beat the 22% growth bar and that cash comes back in fast. Miss it and there's nobody left to catch the knife. $SPX

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Goldman Sachs (Brian Garrett)
items.title: Goldman: hyperscalers may need $5.5-6 trillion by 2030 — the AI trade is becoming a financing story
items.idea: Goldman flagged that hyperscalers could need to raise $5.5 trillion to $6 trillion by 2030 to fund the AI buildout, increasingly leaning on credit markets rather than free cash flow alone. The framing has shifted from a pure growth story to a financing/credit story.
items.angle: Once the AI trade is a credit story, hyperscaler bond spreads become a more honest gauge of sentiment than Nasdaq futures — spreads move on capital discipline questions before equity does.
items.tweetA: Goldman's number: hyperscalers may need to raise $5.5-6 trillion by 2030 to fund the AI buildout. That's not a growth story anymore, that's a credit story. Watch spreads on hyperscaler paper before you watch the Nasdaq.
items.tweetB: $5.5-6T of hyperscaler financing needs by 2030 dwarfs what most credit markets can absorb without repricing risk broadly. Where does that capital come from without cannibalizing every other credit-sensitive trade?

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Goldman Sachs (Brian Garrett)
items.title: Goldman's own flow desk is structuring for a drawdown, not a rally
items.idea: Garrett's preferred trade structures for this "market of stocks": use rich call skew to help finance downside protection, or take a defined, levered bet on a sharp S&P 500 drawdown before the end of August.
items.angle: When the bank's execution desk is pricing cheap drawdown protection into August op-ex rather than chasing the record high, that's a more honest sentiment read than any survey.
items.tweetA: Goldman's own equity execution desk isn't playing for a top, they're playing for insurance — rich call skew to fund downside puts, or a levered bet on a drawdown before end of August. That's a hedge book, not a conviction book.
items.tweetB: When the flow desk is structuring cheap drawdown bets into August expiry instead of chasing the record high, that tells you more about real institutional sentiment than any positioning survey does.

items.title: Iranian drone attack kills 2 US troops; oil round-trips from $68.55 to $80+ in two weeks
items.idea: An Iranian drone attack killed 2 US service members over the weekend, reigniting the conflict just as PPI showed producer price inflation cooling 0.3% MoM. Crude bottomed at $68.55 on July 6 and has since ripped back over $80/barrel on renewed strikes.
items.angle: Lower energy prices were supposed to be the Fed's green light for a cut into a softening labor market. That assumption just broke in the span of two weeks.
items.tweetA: Two US service members killed by an Iranian drone this weekend and crude just round-tripped from $68.55 to $80+ in two weeks. The Fed was counting on cheap energy to justify a cut into a softening labor market. That assumption just broke.
items.tweetB: PPI printed -0.3% MoM, the disinflation story the Fed wants. Then oil ripped ~17% in under two weeks on Iran. Producer prices and crude are now sending opposite signals into the same policy meeting. $USO $XLE

items.title: BofA: Google raises CY26 capex 5% to $190-200B — driven by memory costs, not more chips
items.idea: BofA estimates Google will increase CY26 capex by 5% to $190-200B, specifically due to higher memory costs rather than expanded chip purchases. The market currently isn't rewarding higher capex without clearer AI-ROI visibility — a combination the author flags as a setup for a post-earnings sell-off risk across hyperscalers and semis. 2027 capex is expected to be even higher, following Google's $80B equity raise.
items.angle: An equity raise from a company generating $100B+ in annual free cash flow to fund next year's data centers is the clearest sign yet the capex story is starting to touch the balance sheet, not just the income statement.
items.tweetA: BofA now has Google's 2026 capex at $190-200B, up 5%, and the raise is being driven by memory costs, not more chips. You're paying more for the same compute. That's margin compression dressed up as growth investment.
items.tweetB: Google did an $80B equity raise to help fund 2027 capex. When a company with $100B+ in annual free cash flow needs an equity raise to keep building data centers, the AI capex story is quietly becoming a dilution story. $GOOGL

items.source: Speedrun / P Equity Research (citing Thierry / arvy)
items.title: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket: -32.8% in 3 weeks — only comparable is late 2021, right before the 2022 bear market
items.idea: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket peaked June 22 and is down 32.8% three weeks later. Per Thierry (arvy), the only comparable unwind in the chart's history is late 2021's -34.2% drawdown — which preceded the 2022 bear market. Author sees a possible bottom forming in equipment, photonics and memory, but expects the broader selloff to continue through end of July.
items.angle: This is the single most chart-worthy data point of the batch: a momentum basket replicating the exact setup that preceded the last bear market, unfolding in real time.
items.tweetA: Goldman's High Beta Momentum basket is down 32.8% in three weeks. The only other time this basket moved this fast: late 2021, -34.2%, right before the entire market rolled into a bear cycle. History doesn't repeat, but this chart rhymes uncomfortably.
items.tweetB: If you're only watching the S&P you're missing the actual damage. A basket that just replicated the setup to the 2022 bear market lost a third of its value in 21 days and the index barely blinked. That gap doesn't close quietly.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — Goldman High Beta Momentum basket performance chart showing the -32.8% 3-week drawdown vs. the late-2021 -34.2% comparable (referenced via Thierry/arvy).

items.title: Nasdaq down just 2.47% while individual portfolios are down 30-50%
items.idea: Despite the momentum-basket carnage, the Nasdaq index itself is only down 2.47% over the past two months. The author notes many portfolios holding the names that drove the April-June rally are down 30-50% even as the headline index looks calm — evidence the "buy anything and ride it" trade from April-June is dead.
items.angle: Dispersion, not direction, has been the real trade underneath the tape for weeks — the index-level calm is masking a portfolio-level bloodbath in anything that isn't quality.
items.tweetA: Nasdaq: -2.47% over the past two months. Actual portfolios holding the momentum names that drove 2025-26: down 30-50%. The index is lying to you about what just happened underneath it.
items.tweetB: This is the tell that the April-to-June 'buy anything, ride the momentum' trade is dead: index-level calm, portfolio-level carnage. Dispersion, not direction, was the real 2026 trade and most people are still finding out the hard way.

items.title: SOX down 19% from its peak, RSI near 40 — approaching oversold
items.idea: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) is down 19% from its recent peak with an RSI near 40, closing in on oversold territory, per the author's technical read heading into a heavy week of chip earnings (TXN, INTC, STM, TEL).
items.angle: Combine the SOX drawdown with the momentum basket's -32.8% and you get a semiconductor complex that has already priced in a lot of bad news before Intel and TXN even report.
items.tweetA: SOX is down 19% from its high with an RSI near 40 — getting close to oversold. Combine that with Goldman's momentum basket down a third and you get a semi complex that's already priced a lot of bad news heading into earnings.
items.tweetB: The AI trade's most crowded proxy, semiconductors, is quietly bleeding into oversold while the S&P prints records. Either semis are wrong about the AI cycle, or the index is about to catch up to what semis already know. $SOXX $SMH

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (SOX technical chart).

items.title: Six unrelated valuation frameworks all flag US equities at historically euphoric extremes
items.idea: TMAD examined six different valuation measures, using completely different data and methodology, and reports they all reach the same conclusion: US equities are currently priced at levels seen only during the most euphoric periods in market history. The full breakdown sits behind their paywall (deep-dive report, price increasing from $59/mo to $69/mo on July 21).
items.angle: Six independently-derived valuation extremes landing in the same week as a 32.8% momentum-basket unwind and a 19%-off-highs SOX isn't three unrelated data points — it's the same story told three different ways.
items.tweetA: Six unrelated valuation frameworks, six different methodologies, one conclusion: US equities are priced for the most euphoric periods on record. Meanwhile the momentum basket underneath is already down a third. Valuation extremes and momentum breakdowns showing up in the same week isn't a coincidence.
items.tweetB: Nobody agrees on which valuation model is "right" until all six agree at once. That convergence — not any single multiple — is the actual signal here.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (six-metric valuation comparison, SPX algo levels, GEX-by-strike positioning chart).

items.title: Kalshi odds of Hormuz traffic normalizing by Sept 1 crashed from 69% to 7% in under a month
items.idea: Kalshi contract odds that Strait of Hormuz traffic would normalize by Sept 1 peaked at 69% on June 25 and have collapsed to just 7% today — the lowest reading since the contract started (with only a 41% chance of normalization even by year-end). The author had flagged two weeks ago that the prior detente could break on either "Iran pushing beyond current state of play" or "kinetic conflict escalating beyond tit-for-tat" — both have now happened.
items.angle: A prediction market repricing an entire regional supply chain by 62 points in three weeks is a cleaner, faster-moving signal than anything coming out of oil futures or equity vol right now.
items.tweetA: A prediction market is the cleanest read on this war right now. Kalshi odds that Hormuz traffic normalizes by Sept 1: 69% on June 25, 7% today. That's not sentiment drifting — that's a market repricing an entire regional supply chain in three weeks.
items.tweetB: Forget the oil price for a second and look at the Kalshi curve on Hormuz normalization: 69% to 7%. If that market is right, every crude forecast built on "this settles down by Q3" needs to be thrown out. $USO $BNO

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — Kalshi Hormuz-normalization-by-Sept-1 contract price collapsing from 69% (Jun 25) to 7% (Jul 19).

items.title: UK gets a new PM Monday — Labour's Andy Burnham — right into a still-sticky inflation print
items.idea: Andy Burnham becomes the UK's new Prime Minister on Monday. The transition lands the same week as June UK inflation data, where the house economist forecasts headline CPI slowing to 2.70% y/y, core at 2.55%, and services CPI at 3.45% — still elevated. June labour market indicators land Tuesday and retail sales/GfK confidence on Friday.
items.angle: Nobody is pricing UK political risk right now because all eyes are on Hormuz and the Fed — but a leadership transition landing mid-inflation-fight is exactly the kind of story gilt markets notice before equity desks do.
items.tweetA: The UK gets a new PM Monday, Andy Burnham, right as the market expects headline CPI to slow to 2.70% and services CPI to stay elevated at 3.45%. A leadership change on top of a still-sticky services print is exactly the setup gilt vol doesn't like.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Hormuz and the Fed this week. Nobody's pricing that the UK just changed Prime Ministers mid-inflation-fight. That's the kind of story that gets ignored until gilts move on it. $GBP

items.title: Second-heaviest earnings week of the season: 88 SPX names, 18% of index earnings weight
items.idea: This week brings 88 S&P 500 components reporting, representing 18% of SPX earnings weight — the second-heaviest week of the season. That includes two Mag-7 names (GOOGL, TSLA) plus 21 other companies with market caps over $100bn: INTC, PM, GEV, TXN, RTX, AXP, TMUS, IBM, TMO, NEE, VZ, SCHW, UNP, BX, IBKR, T, DHR, CB, COF, LMT, NOW. Ex-US: SK Hynix, SAP, Dassault Systemes also report.
items.angle: Everyone will be watching GOOGL and TSLA, but the real macro tell this week is in the other 21 names — RTX, LMT, IBM, DHR, T — because that's industrials, defense, and telecom earnings quality, not just AI-capex commentary.
items.tweetA: 88 S&P 500 companies report this week, 18% of the index's earnings weight, including two Mag-7 names. This is the week that decides whether "earnings justify the multiple" survives contact with reality.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching GOOGL and TSLA, but there are 21 other >$100B companies reporting the same five days: RTX, LMT, IBM, DHR, T. That's the real macro read this week — industrials and defense earnings quality, not just Mag-7 AI capex.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (extensive BofA/DB earnings, positioning, breadth, gamma, and sentiment cheat sheets referenced throughout).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The oil threshold framework: $90-93 is a tax, $100 is a Fed problem, $110+ is a global shock
items.idea: The author lays out a clear escalation framework: oil at $88-93 (current levels, Brent breaching $90) acts as a slow tax on consumers and margins that markets can absorb. Above $100 it becomes much harder for central banks and equity investors to ignore. Above $110 — especially if driven by measurable lost barrels rather than headlines — oil stops behaving like a regional war premium and starts behaving like a global economic shock.
items.angle: This is a specific, falsifiable framework, not vague "oil is a risk" commentary — it gives exact levels to watch rather than just a direction.
items.tweetA: There's a real threshold framework worth remembering here: oil below $100 is a slow tax markets absorb. Above $100, central banks can't ignore it. Above $110, especially on real lost barrels rather than headlines, it stops being a war premium and starts being a global shock. We're at $88-93 now.
items.tweetB: The market isn't pricing a supply shock right now, it's pricing a supply scare. The gap between those two things is about $20 of crude — and it's the whole ballgame for whether the Fed's next move is a cut or a hold.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Iran strikes Kuwaiti power and desalination facilities — a shift from military signaling to civilian life-support systems
items.idea: Iran's attacks over the weekend expanded to Kuwaiti power and desalination facilities, which the author frames as a meaningful escalation beyond military signaling — water infrastructure is not symbolic in the Gulf, it's foundational to civilian economic function. The piece frames "conflict broadening materially" (Israel re-entering, direct hits on Gulf production, sustained civilian-infrastructure campaigns) as one of two key escalation doors, alongside Hormuz becoming physically impassable.
items.angle: Markets are pricing this purely as an energy/inflation story. Desalination and power-grid strikes in Kuwait are the actual leading indicator for whether this stays regional or goes systemic — and that distinction isn't showing up in oil or equity pricing yet.
items.tweetA: Iran hitting Kuwaiti desalination plants is a bigger tell than another missile exchange. Water infrastructure in the Gulf isn't symbolic, it's existential. Once the target set moves from military to civilian life-support systems, "contained conflict" stops being the base case.
items.tweetB: Markets are pricing this as an energy story. Desalination and power-grid strikes in Kuwait are actually the leading indicator for whether this stays regional or goes systemic — and that's not showing up in oil or equity vol yet.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The AI debate has quietly shifted from "is it real" to "how much capital gets burned before it's profitable"
items.idea: The piece argues equity investors were happy to pay for distant AI upside, while credit investors are increasingly focused on issuance, leverage, and shrinking coverage ratios. Hyperscalers once viewed as pristine balance sheets are becoming some of the largest borrowers in the market, while chip and hardware companies stay exposed to any capex slowdown.
items.angle: This directly reinforces the separate $5.5-6T Goldman financing figure from earlier tonight — two independent sources converging on the same read: the AI trade's next phase is a credit story, not an equity story.
items.tweetA: The AI debate quietly changed shape. It's not "is this real" anymore, it's "how much capital gets burned before the economics work." Hyperscalers with supposedly pristine balance sheets are turning into some of the largest borrowers in the entire market.
items.tweetB: First phase of the AI boom paid everyone standing near the shovel factory. Second phase is going to be far more selective about who actually captures the economics — and credit markets, not equity markets, will be the ones enforcing that discipline.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: South Korea's KOSPI reopening is the real-time test of whether the AI/semi washout found a floor
items.idea: The author frames South Korea's KOSPI reopening after Friday's holiday as a critical bellwether — it combines heavy chip/memory concentration, foreign ownership, and leveraged retail products, making it less a local equity index and more a global scoreboard for AI hardware and momentum positioning. A contained reopen would support the "exhausted forced selling" thesis; a fresh downside gap would suggest more forced selling remains.
items.angle: This is the single cleanest near-term tell for whether Friday's global tech selloff is over — and it's getting far less attention than Nasdaq futures.
items.tweetA: Forget Nasdaq futures tonight. The real tell on whether the AI unwind is over is the KOSPI reopen — heaviest chip concentration, most foreign ownership, most leveraged retail exposure of any major index. It's the global proxy for AI momentum, not the S&P.
items.tweetB: If Korea gaps down on the reopen, the AI washout isn't finished. If it holds, Friday's global tech selloff exhausted the forced selling. Almost nobody outside Asia desks is watching this the way they should be. $EWY

items.title: Russia fires 41 missiles at Kyiv in largest ballistic barrage on the capital since the war began
items.idea: Russia hit Kyiv overnight with a 41-missile barrage over roughly 5 hours, killing at least 1 and wounding 16 — described by Ukrainian officials as one of the largest ballistic missile attacks on the capital since the full-scale invasion began. The strike lands as Ukraine's own leadership faces internal turmoil, with 3 consecutive days of protests over the dismissal of former defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov and criticism of Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi.
items.angle: This is happening in real time and getting almost no risk-premium reaction in Western markets — the gap between the actual escalation and how little vol it's carrying is worth tracking.
items.tweetA: 41 missiles, 5 hours, the largest ballistic barrage on Kyiv since the war began — and it's barely moving Western markets. The disconnect between what's actually happening in this war and how little risk premium it carries keeps widening.
items.tweetB: Russia just fired its largest missile barrage on Kyiv since the war started, the same weekend Iran killed two US troops and hit Kuwaiti infrastructure. Three separate escalation fronts in 72 hours and equity vol barely moved. Either markets are right that none of this is systemic, or complacency just got very expensive.

items.title: Ukraine's actual constraint isn't launchers — it's Patriot interceptor inventory
items.idea: The piece argues ballistic missiles compress the defense timeline and force Patriot-class batteries into fast, expensive intercept decisions; the binding constraint is interceptor ammunition, resupply cadence, and crew willingness to expend scarce rounds against mixed salvos designed to drain magazines. Zelenskyy has requested 300 Patriot systems ahead of winter.
items.angle: The number that actually matters isn't the 300-Patriot ask, it's the monthly manufacturing/shipping rate of interceptors — that production rate, not launcher counts, is the real war-of-attrition metric.
items.tweetA: The actual constraint in Ukraine right now isn't launchers, it's ammunition. Zelenskyy wants 300 Patriot systems before winter, but the number that matters is how many interceptors NATO can manufacture and ship monthly. That production rate is the whole war.
items.tweetB: Russia's saturation tactic is straightforward: mix missile types to force expensive intercept decisions and drain magazines faster than allies can resupply. This is an attrition war on interceptor inventories now, not a war of maneuver.
